3:
The twelfth doctor in three years.

Marina sits in the waiting room, a pale leaf pressed between the pages of a glossy magazine. She has learned to arrive early, to fill out the new-patient forms with the weary precision of a scribe copying a familiar, hopeless text. Name, date of birth, symptoms. The list is long now, a cascading column of small misfires: fatigue like wet cement in the veins, a brain wrapped in thick felt, a throat that is not sore but somehow perpetually occupied.

She offers her body, again, as a document for interpretation. Each doctor will add a marginal note, a correction in a different hand. None will read it to the end. She waits, clutching the file that holds her history—a thin, growing testament to everything her body has become, and a silent, shrinking record of all it has buried.
4: Marina sits in the endocrinologist’s waiting room, staring at her referral. “Subclinical hypothyroidism. Dynamic observation.” This is what they called a diagnosis. It means: we see something is wrong, but we don’t know what, and for now, we’re just watching.
5: Waiting, she opens the manuscript she is taking home for the weekend—a book about ancient medicine. The first page reads: "Hippocrates did not ask what hurts. He asked how the patient lives." Marina reads it again. Then she looks at the office door. In three years, not a single doctor has asked her how she lives.
6:
Perhaps it wasn’t the thyroid at all.

Marina sat in the clinic, holding the printout of her test results. The numbers were neatly arranged in their columns, each one a tiny verdict. Her body was a collection of symptoms, a landscape of fatigue and chill, but the diagnosis felt like a label slapped on a crate whose contents remained unknown. The doctor had spoken of antibodies and hormones, of a sluggish gland. But as she listened, a quiet, stubborn thought took root: what if the gland was only the messenger? What if it was speaking for something else entirely—something older, more entrenched, that no blood test could quantify? She folded the paper, the crease sharp and final. The origin, she sensed, was not a single point on a chart. It was a story.
62: Marina sorted through her mother’s belongings after the move to the care home. In an old folder, she found a medical record. 1987. “Asthenic syndrome. Chronic fatigue. Rest recommended.”
63: Her mother was forty-two at the time. Marina is now forty-four. That year, her mother abandoned her correspondence postgraduate studies. “My health won’t allow it,” she would explain. And she never returned to academia again.
64: Marina stared at the card for a long time. Then she took out her smartphone and typed: ‘can fatigue be inherited’. She did not yet know that the answer to this question would change how she understood not only her mother’s illness, but her own.
119: At a consultation with a new specialist—an integrative therapist her friend had recommended—Marina heard an unexpected question: “When was the last time you felt truly hungry?”
120: Marina opens her mouth to answer—and cannot. She does not remember. She eats by the clock. Hunger, the body’s signal—she has not heard it in years.
121: "And anything else, besides pain and fatigue?" the doctor continues. Marina is silent. Three years—and only two signals from her body. Something had been lost long before the illness.
176:
TSH—4.2. Normal is up to 4.0.

The number was a quiet trespass, a barely perceptible step over a line drawn in invisible ink. For the doctor, it was a non-event, a statistical ghost. For Marina, it was a verdict. It gave a name, a measurable shape, to the fog that had filled her for years. The fatigue was no longer a moral failing, a lack of will. It was a chemical fact. A part of her, a cowardly part, felt a perverse relief. Here was the proof. She was not lazy; she was ill. The body had produced its own alibi, a perfect, clinical excuse to stay silent, to keep the blank page blank.
177: The endocrinologist says, “A borderline value. We’ll monitor it.” Marina looks at the printout. A value of 4.2—this is not a diagnosis, not a treatment, not an explanation for why she wakes up already tired.
178: On the way home she thinks: a symptom must mean something, mustn't it? The body is saying something—but what? What is it saying with this fatigue that nothing can explain?
179: She did not yet know how to read this language. This chapter was her first lesson.
227: Marina keeps a journal. On her therapist’s advice—to write down everything she notices about how she feels, and everything that happens in her life alongside it.
228: Three weeks later, she notices something strange. The fatigue does not worsen after physical exertion. It worsens after conversations about plans. After meetings with the authors whose manuscripts she edits. After evenings when the apartment is quiet—when, theoretically, she could sit down and write.
229: It seemed senseless to her. Why the fatigue—when it was quiet? When there was an opportunity? She didn’t understand. But she wrote it down.
286: So it's because of my mother? Marina asks. She has read about epigenetics and looks at the doctor with an expression in which relief and anger are mixed.
287: It’s not because of your mother, the therapist says. It’s through your mother. These are different things.
288: "What's the difference?" "'Because of' means your mother is to blame. 'Through' means it is the path by which the information reached you. Epigenetic marks can be changed. Not all of them, and not at once. But they can."
289: The idea that she herself could alter her own biology seemed to Marina both incredible and terrifying.
328: The therapist suggests an experiment: each evening for a week, spend five minutes simply sitting and asking the body, “What are you feeling right now?” Without judgment. Just asking.
329: On the third day, Marina notices something strange. The first thing her body offers is a tightness in her chest. Not pain. Something more like restraint. As if something is being held inside, not allowed to escape.
330: She notices that this tightness always comes at the same time—late in the evening, when the children are asleep and the apartment is quiet. When, theoretically, she could be writing. The body knows something she does not yet know.
376:
A friend brings Marina to an acupuncturist. "Try it. It helped me."

The room smells of moxa and quiet. The acupuncturist, a woman with calm hands, speaks of qi, of the substance of life, of blocked rivers in the body. Marina lies on the table, a specimen pinned under sterile needles. Each insertion is a precise, distant sting.

She thinks of the ancient concepts—energy, flow, meridians. Then she thinks of her own modern substance: antibodies, receptors, lab results in neat columns. Two languages for the same silence inside her. The needles hum a faint, metallic song against her skin. She holds perfectly still, a bridge between two worlds, afraid the slightest tremor might prove both of them wrong.
377: Marina lies with the needles and thinks: this is unscientific. Meridians do not exist. She has edited enough medical books to know this.
378: And yet, twenty minutes later, something happens. A warmth that spreads from the point on her wrist up her arm. A lightness in her shoulders, which she had not felt for months.
379:
"Where does it come from?" she asks later.
"We don't know for certain. There are several hypotheses. Would you like to understand?"
Marina would.
This chapter is a candid conversation about what science knows, what it is investigating, and what remains, for now, an open question.
421:
Marina compares the two doctors she saw this week.

The first, a young endocrinologist, spoke in protocols and numbers. He looked at her chart, not at her. His office smelled of antiseptic and printed forms. He prescribed a precise dosage, explained the mechanism of action in a clear, hollow voice, and sent her on her way. A mechanic for a faulty gland.

The second, an older therapist with tired eyes, asked about her sleep. He listened to the silence between her answers. His office held the scent of old books and wood. He said nothing about antibodies, but spoke of the body’s memory, of how it sometimes takes on burdens the soul can no longer carry. He offered no prescription, only a question that lingered in the air after she left.

Who treats the illness, and who treats the person? The method, or the one who holds it?
422: The first one: he stares at the screen while she speaks. Prints out a referral. “Next.”
423: The second one—her therapist: looks at her. When she speaks, he leans slightly forward. When she falls silent mid-sentence—he waits. He does not fill the silence.
424: After the first appointment, Marina feels worse than before. After the second, a little better. The prescriptions are the same. The information identical. But something in the contact itself is different. It’s not mysticism. It’s measurable.
462: On the channel “A Little Bit of Everything,” Marina listens to a physicist talking about a book on systems thinking. “When you look at the parts, you cannot see the whole,” he says. “Water is not oxygen plus hydrogen. The whole is always greater than the sum of its parts.”
463: Marina thinks about her test results. Her thyroid is almost normal. Her heart is normal. Each individual part—almost normal. But as a whole—she is not normal. She is tired. She is not living.
464:
Perhaps the problem wasn’t in any one part?

Marina sat in the clinic corridor, the diagnosis—Hashimoto’s—now a cold fact in her hands. For years she had presented her symptoms in pieces: the fatigue, the brain fog, the weight gain, the chill. Each specialist examined their fragment, prescribed for their system. The endocrinologist for the thyroid. The neurologist for the nerves. The cardiologist for the pulse.

But what if the illness was not a list of broken components? What if it was the pattern of cracks itself? The body, refusing to be divided into departments. A whole, silent protest.

She thought of her unfinished manuscript, abandoned in a drawer. Not a flawed chapter, not a weak character. A failure of the entire story. She had treated her life the same way—compartmentalized, never assessing the whole. The thought was terrifying. And, for the first time, complete.
508:
The therapist attaches a small sensor to Marina’s wrist. “We’re measuring your heart rate variability. It’s a window into the state of your nervous system.”

The screen blooms with a jagged, luminous graph. A frantic, spiking line—her sympathetic system, a chronic state of alert. A faint, shallow wave beneath it—the parasympathetic, the rest-and-digest, barely a whisper.

Marina watches the two lines, the stark topography of her inner life. The body keeps its own ledger, she thinks. It records every deferred dream, every swallowed word, as a physiological fact. This frantic peak is the fear she feels before a blank page. This shallow trough is the exhaustion that follows, the retreat.

The machine confirms what she already knows: she is living in a state of perpetual preparation for a fight she never actually fights.
509: Five minutes later, a graph appeared on the screen. “Your nervous system is operating in a state of chronic readiness. Not for a specific danger—just in a mode of ‘something might happen.’ This is very costly for the body.”
510: Since when? It was difficult to say precisely. But such patterns are not usually formed in a matter of weeks.
511: Marina remembers herself at twenty-three. Three envelopes bearing rejections. Her mother’s voice. Something she decided then—and has never revisited since.
561: The blood test shows elevated inflammatory markers—slightly, "within the upper limit of normal." The rheumatologist says, "It's not critical. Just monitor it."
562: Marina asks her therapist, “What does it mean—chronic inflammation at that level?”—“The immune system is constantly a little activated. Like an alarm that won’t turn off. And it’s linked to the state of the nervous system, the hormonal background, the quality of sleep—everything is in conversation with everything else.”
563: “And why is it activated?” The therapist looks at her. “Usually, because the body perceives a threat. A real one, or one it believes to be real.”
564: Marina does not yet understand the threat in her life. But she will.
623: Marina receives the results of her microbiome test. A long list of bacteria, deviations, recommendations arrives by email. “I thought I would understand myself better,” she says. “It only became more complicated.”
624: At the appointment, the therapist says something unexpected: “The intestine is literally the border between you and the world. Nine meters where the external becomes internal. And the state of that border depends greatly on how open you are to the world—or how much you defend yourself from it.”
625:
Marina thinks: she is defending herself. She has been defending herself for many years. But from what?

From the world? From others? No, that is too simple. She defends herself from herself. From the hand that might reach for a pen. From the thought that might crystallize into a sentence. Her body, this dense, tired fortress, has been built to keep the most dangerous thing locked safely inside: her own voice.

The silence within her is not empty. It is a populated, teeming silence. A microbial city of fears, regrets, and shelved beginnings. They communicate in their own chemical language, these tenants of her inner world, and their consensus is clear: stay quiet, stay safe. The gates must remain closed.
669:
Have you ever noticed that your fatigue is selective? That for some things you have energy, and for others you have none?

Marina noticed. For the tedious line at the clinic, for the small talk at the office, for the grocery store after work—her energy was a flat, drained zero. But for reading a manuscript late into the night, for the intricate analysis of a sentence’s rhythm, for the silent, fierce joy of a perfectly chosen word—for that, a reserve would mysteriously appear. It was as if her metabolism, her very energy exchange, was not a public utility, but a private, fiercely guarded treasury. It funded only what it deemed worthy. It was an architecture built for a single, neglected purpose.
670:
Marina thinks. Yes. For work—there is energy. For the children—there is energy. But for...
She stops.
671: On what? A pause. On myself. Not as a mother or an editor. On myself—there is no energy.
672: The body allocates its resources with great precision. Sometimes, it protects us precisely by withholding those resources from a particular thing.
673:
Marina writes it down in her notebook. Underlines it twice.

Metabolism is not just digestion. It is the architecture of life. The transformation of matter into energy, energy into matter. A constant, invisible exchange between the body and the world. A slow, meticulous construction of oneself, cell by cell.

She thinks of her own architecture. The foundation laid in childhood, the hasty additions of youth, the faulty wiring of stress. Now, the whole structure feels drafty, unstable. The energy exchange is broken. The world comes in, but nothing is transformed. Nothing builds.

She closes her eyes. For a moment, she imagines a different blueprint. A design not for endurance, but for creation. A structure with space for light.
725: Marina reads the study: in people with chronic anxiety, the level of inflammatory markers is perpetually elevated. Psychological state and immunity—this is not a metaphor. It is biochemistry.
726: So, what happens in her head literally happens in her blood. Her unhealing something—it’s not in her head. It is everywhere. In every cell.
727: And so the question shifts. Not, "What is wrong with my thyroid?" But: "What is happening in my life that my body is keeping itself in a state of constant alarm?"
728: The answer begins to surface. She is not yet ready to see it.
767: Marina notices a pattern: she feels worst on Sunday evenings. Not Monday morning. On Sunday, when nothing has even begun yet.
768: What happens on Sunday evening? Nothing. The children do their homework, her husband watches something. Silence. And in that silence—what?
769: A long pause. "I think there could be an evening for something. And I am too tired. And I'm angry with myself for it."—"For what, exactly?"—"For not writing."
770: It is the first time she has said it aloud. She is surprised herself to have spoken.
821: The body is constantly sending signals, the therapist explains. Sometimes that signal reaches the conscious mind as a sensation, an intuition, an image. Most people have learned to ignore it.
822: “How did we learn?”—“Sometimes because in childhood, the body’s signals weren’t respected. ‘Don’t cry.’ ‘Don’t make things up.’ ‘Everything’s fine.’ The body learns to be silent.”
823: Marina hears her mother’s voice: “Don’t make things up.” It was her standard reply to everything. To pain, to fear, to desires.
824: So, the body had truly learned to be silent. But silence is not an absence of signal. It is a signal that has gone deeper.
868: Imagine your genome is a text. And the epigenome is how that text is read: what is emphasized, what is in parentheses, what is crossed out. The text does not change. But the meaning—is profoundly different.
869: Marina thinks: this is precisely what she does. Editing is not rewriting a text. It is altering the way a text is read.
870:
"And this epigenome—can it be changed?"—"Yes. It takes time. But yes."

She walked home through the park, the words a quiet drumbeat in her mind. *It takes time.* The autumn leaves, crisp and amber, were not simply falling; they were in a state of long, patient transformation. Her body, with its weary thyroid, its silent rebellion, was not a fixed sentence. It was a record, a manuscript written not only by her ancestors but by her own life—by every sleepless night of worry, every swallowed word, every dream filed away in a drawer marked *someday*. The air was cold and clear. She felt, for the first time in years, not like a prisoner of her own cells, but like their editor.
871: Marina looks at her hands. The hands of an editor of other people's texts. She wonders: is it possible to edit what life has written upon her?
910:
Stress isn't always bad, the therapist says. Small stress trains the system. The problem is when stress is chronic and there is no recovery.

Marina listens, her fingers tracing the edge of the notebook in her lap. She thinks of her body as a system under a slow, unending siege. No acute battles, just a permanent, quiet occupation. The fatigue, the fog, the heavy limbs—these are not a failure to fight, but a profound, cellular adaptation. A lowering of all drawbridges. A body learning to endure a siege by becoming the siege itself.
911: “And what counts as recovery?” — “That which truly nourishes. Not passive rest with a phone. But that after which you have more, not less.”
912: Marina thinks. What nourishes her? Music—yes. Good prose—yes. "And does writing nourish you?" asks the therapist. He has asked this before, more than once.
913:
I don't know, says Marina. I haven't tried in a long time.

She says it to the therapist, whose office smells of dust and dried lavender. The question was simple: what brings you joy? Marina looks at her own hands in her lap. The silence stretches, thin and brittle. She realizes she is examining the question not as a feeling to be recalled, but as a theoretical concept, a phenomenon described in a book she might edit. A distant country whose language she once spoke, but has now forgotten. The path to it is overgrown. She has not tried to find it. She has, in fact, carefully avoided all the signposts.
959:
Marina reads about the inhabitants of Okinawa. They have an ‘ikigai’—a reason to get up in the morning. A purpose that does not end with retirement.

Her own mornings begin with a dense, chemical fog and the weight of the duvet. Her reason is the alarm clock, the need to earn a living, the automatic motion of brewing coffee. A series of obligations, not a purpose.

She closes the book and looks at her hands resting on the cover. They are capable hands, editor’s hands, always shaping the words of others. The question forms quietly, a faint echo in the stillness of her apartment: what do these hands want to build for themselves? The fog in her head seems to thin, just for a moment, letting a sliver of sharp, unfamiliar light through.
960:
“Do you have an ikigai?” she asks herself. Half-joking. But the answer comes seriously.

No. She does not have a reason for being. She has reasons for enduring. To get through the day. To not upset her mother. To finish editing the manuscript by Tuesday. To endure is not to live, she thinks. It is to postpone death. A postponed death is a kind of life, perhaps. A life in the conditional mood. A life of ‘if only’ and ‘maybe later.’ Her ikigai, if it exists, is hidden beneath the sediment of these postponements. Like a lost earring under a layer of dust. She does not have the strength to sweep the floor. So she endures that, too.
961: Children? Yes, but they will leave soon. Work? No—work is an obligation, not a calling. Something of her own?
962: She turns away from the thought faster than it can fully form. A therapist once told her, "A sense of meaning predicts life expectancy more reliably than most medical markers." Marina had nodded then. Now she thinks of it differently.
1018: “I want to understand,” says Marina. “Not just take pills and wait. I want to understand what is happening, and what I can do about it.”
1019: The therapist smiles. “This is called an activated patient. It changes treatment outcomes—statistically significant.”
1020: “Seriously?”—“When a person understands their illness and participates in the decisions, the outcomes are better. Not because they are smarter than the doctor. Because their body knows things about itself that a doctor cannot know. And the patient begins to use that knowledge together with the doctor.”
1021: Marina thinks: for twenty years she has been a passive patient. For twenty years—a passive participant in her own life. Perhaps it’s the same thing?
1070:
When you fell ill—what was happening in your life?

The question, posed by a new endocrinologist, hung in the air. Marina stared at the pale wall behind him. The usual script involved lists of symptoms, dates, dosages. Not this. Not a bridge thrown from her body back to her life.

What was happening? The same as always. Manuscripts. Deadlines. The silent, accusing blank page on her own desk. The slow retreat from friends, from light, from any thought that felt too much like a beginning.

But under the doctor’s quiet gaze, the chronology shifted. The first crushing fatigue did not arrive after a virus. It settled in the week she finally deleted the folder labeled “Novel_Notes,” emptying the recycle bin with a decisive click. The tremor in her hands began not with a hormone shift, but the morning she read a brilliant debut by a writer twenty years her junior.

The illness had not interrupted her life. It had become its most accurate record.
1071: Marina thinks. Three years ago. Her husband landed a major project, the children moved up to high school. She began working fewer evenings. Time appeared.
1072: "And?"—"And... nothing, really. The fatigue just appeared. I thought it was something seasonal."
1073: And before the fatigue—what did you feel when the time appeared? A long pause. Anxiety. I didn’t know what to do with that time.
1074: Do you understand now? Marina looks out the window. I am beginning.
1128: The therapist places a large diagram on the table. "This is a matrix. We look at you systemically: body, psyche, history, environment, meaning. We are not searching for what is broken. We are observing how the system is organized."
1129: Marina looks at the diagram. It resembles editing—when you see not the individual words, but the entire structure of the text. The lines connect her cortisol to her insomnia, her antibodies to the fog in her brain, her abandoned novel to the tightness in her throat. It is a map of cascading failures. For so long, each symptom lived in isolation, a lonely, inexplicable fact. Now, they are linked in a coherent, devastating narrative. Her body has been a manuscript, written in the language of fatigue and fear. And she, its reluctant editor, is finally beginning to understand its grammar.
1130: "Alright," she says. "Let's do it. But I want to understand every single step."
1131: "That is precisely the way," the therapist replies. "Diagnosis is not something done to you. It is something you do together."
1204: This time, Marina arrives with a notebook. She has decided to record not only the answers, but also the questions.
1205: You know what makes a good listener? It’s not that they find answers quickly. It’s that they know how to tolerate incompleteness. To remain within the question, without rushing toward the answer.
1206: Marina thinks: this is something she was never taught. At the editorial office—speed, deadlines, resolution. In childhood—"don't make things up," close the matter. She was never taught to live with an open question.
1207: “And can you live with unanswered questions?” “I’m learning,” says Marina.
1258: The therapist asks her to recall three moments from childhood when something important—a desire, a feeling, an impulse—was simply stopped. Just stopped.
1259: The first memory that surfaces: she is seven years old, she has written a poem and shows it to her mother. Her mother reads it, makes corrections, and says: "There, now it's better." But it was no longer her poem.
1260: Second: at fifteen, she kept a diary. Her mother found it. ‘Why write all this nonsense.’
1261: Third: at twenty-three—three rejections of her book. Never tried again.
1262:
“What do you feel when you remember all this?”—“Fatigue,” says Marina.

She sits in the therapist’s office, a biographical health card on her lap. It is a list. Childhood: frequent sore throats. Adolescence: migraines before exams. University: insomnia before deadlines. The first editorial job: gastritis during a major project. The abandoned manuscript: a mono-like exhaustion that lasted a year.

Each entry is a small, quiet rebellion of the body, a protest she never heard. She had called it life, this chain of ailments. She had thought it normal to carry this weight.

Now, looking at the chronology laid out like evidence, she sees it. Not a series of misfortunes, but a pattern. A biography written not in words, but in symptoms.
1310:
“Tell me about anger,” the therapist says.

Marina looks out the window. The sky is the color of a faded bruise. Anger? She doesn’t get angry. She gets tired. A profound, cellular fatigue that turns her limbs to clay. When her boss dismisses her idea, she feels a wave of heat, then a familiar hollowing in her chest. By evening, her throat is tight, her shoulders stone.

The therapist waits. The silence in the room grows dense.

“I don’t feel anger,” Marina repeats, her voice thin. “I feel… unwell.”

She traces the edge of the armchair. A memory surfaces: her manuscript returned, unread, twenty years ago. The sharp, bright sting of it. Then, nothing. A great, swallowing nothing. That was when the first fatigue settled in, a soft, smothering blanket.

She looks at her hands in her lap. They are perfectly still. The connection forms, delicate and terrifying: the rejected book, the swallowed feeling, the body that agreed to carry the weight.
1311:
"I'm not angry," Marina replies automatically.

The therapist waits. The silence in the room grows heavy, a physical presence. Marina shifts in her chair, feels the familiar ache in her neck, the tight band across her shoulders. She thinks of the editorial meeting that morning, the condescending smile of the new publisher, her own polite, swallowed-down words.

Her hand moves to her throat, to the tender, swollen gland beneath her skin. A lump she carries always.

"I'm not angry," she repeats, but the words sound hollow now, even to her. The tightness in her shoulders speaks a different, more honest language. The body, it seems, keeps its own meticulous records, writing down every slight, every "no," every silenced protest in a script of tension and fatigue. It remembers what the mind so diligently forgets.
1312: "Never?" A pause. "Well... sometimes. But I don't show it. It's unprofessional." "And at home?" "At home, I'm a mother. A mother doesn't show anger."
1313:
And when anger stays inside—where does it go?

It doesn’t evaporate. Marina knows this now. It settles. It chooses a place, a weak point, a fault line in the geography of the body. It becomes a low, persistent ache in the neck, a stiffness in the shoulders, a clenched jaw in the morning. It is a knot under the ribs, a heavy stone in the gut.

Her body, it turns out, is a meticulous archivist. It files every slight, every swallowed protest, every ‘it’s fine’ that was a lie. The files are not paper; they are inflammation. They are fatigue. They are a thyroid gland, besieged and weary, turning its own defenses into a slow, cold war against itself.

The bridge is real. And she is walking across it, one heavy step at a time.
1314: Marina stays silent. The anger—has it simply… gone? But then she understands: it hasn’t gone anywhere. It has stayed. In her body.
1315:
Into the body, she says quietly. Yes, says the therapist.

It is not a metaphor. It is a topography. A map of retreat. When a thought became too sharp, a hope too bare, she learned to draw it inward, away from the visible world, and bury it in the soft, forgiving flesh. A disappointment settled in the shoulders. A forgotten dream calcified in the joints. Shame, hot and quick, found a home in the gut. Yearning, with nowhere to go, thickened the blood and slowed the heart.

She had built a citadel within herself, brick by brick, ache by ache. Now she stands at its gate, key in hand, afraid of the silence inside.
1370: Sometimes illness is the most intelligent thing the body could have done. It gives something. Or protects from something. If you simply remove the illness—without understanding what it was protecting you from—the body will find another way.
1371: It sounds like I want to be sick, Marina says with irritation.
1372: You do not want it. But a part of you, perhaps, needs what the illness provides.
1373: Marina is angry. Then she thinks. Then she says, slowly: "The fatigue keeps me from writing. If it weren't for the fatigue... I suppose I would have to try. That is more frightening than the fatigue."
1374:
A long silence.

It was not the silence of an empty room, but the dense, woolen silence of a body that has turned inward. Marina sat at her desk, the blank document a white desert on the screen. Her fingers, resting on the keys, were cold. A familiar, leaden fatigue began to pool in her limbs, a viscous tide rising just as the first word formed, ghost-like, in her mind. It was an automatic response, a physiological veto. Her shoulders crept toward her ears; a dull ache announced itself at the base of her skull. The body barricaded itself, a fortress refusing to surrender its prisoner. It would rather be sick, it seemed, than be seen.
1419: Marina finally says it out loud: “I’m afraid to write and be rejected again. I’m afraid my mother was right—that I’m not a writer. That if I try and fail, I won’t survive it.”
1420: She speaks and she cries. For the first time in many months.
1421: The therapist is silent. Then she says, “It’s not weakness. It’s your system’s precise assessment of what is dangerous for it. Your body is protecting you from what it considers lethal.”
1422: "But it's not fatal—to get a rejection."—"For your model of the world, it is fatal. For that model of the world that remembers you at seven, at fifteen, at twenty-three."
1469: I want to start writing, says Marina. But every time I sit down, nothing comes out. Or I find something urgent to do. Or I fall asleep.
1470: This is not laziness. This is your nervous system protecting you from what it perceives as a threat. Your brain has learned: writing equals danger. It is a neural pathway. It can be changed—but not through willpower. Through small steps that forge a new association: writing equals safety.
1471: “What steps?” “Begin with what doesn’t frighten you. Ten minutes. Nothing obligatory. Just your hand and the paper.”
1530: Marina reads Viktor Frankl. "Meaning is not created. It is discovered." She writes in her notebook: "What I am looking for already exists. I simply haven't allowed myself to find it."
1531: At her next appointment: "I realized something. For twenty years, I've been editing other people's voices because I'm afraid of my own. But my voice never went away. It was just waiting."
1532: “And what will you do with that?”—“I don’t know yet. But it’s become a little easier, just from having said it.”
1533:
This is important, he says. Sometimes to name a thing is already half the journey.

She sits in the therapist’s chair, a blanket over her knees, and lets the words settle. Hashimoto’s. Autoimmunity. Chronic fatigue. For years they were a verdict, a sentence passed by indifferent doctors in white coats. Now, in this quiet room, they feel different. They are not just the name of a disease, but the name of her condition. Her state of being.

To name it is to separate it from herself, if only by a millimeter. It is no longer a fog she is lost within, but a weather system she can observe. It has borders. It has a history. And if it has a beginning, perhaps it can have an end. The fear of writing, the silence of twenty years—these, too, wait for their names.
1577: Marina changes her diet. Less sugar, more varied vegetables, fatty fish once a week. A month later, she notices: the fog in her head has thinned.
1578: “You mean food can do that?” “Food affects inflammation, the microbiome, the synthesis of neurotransmitters. It’s not a cure. But it’s a way of lessening the load. When the load is lighter, the body finds its own resources.”
1579: Resources. Strength. Marina thinks about that word. She needs strength—for something she is still afraid to name directly.
1626: For the first time in a long while, Marina wakes without an alarm. She lies still for several minutes: the usual feeling of ‘must get up and run’ is absent. There is only silence.
1627: She writes in her diary: "This morning after waking, I did not think. About anything. I simply lay there. It is strange. I am accustomed to thoughts beginning at once, the moment I open my eyes."
1628: Marina thinks: she has been skimping on sleep for years. She believed that time could be used more productively. More productively—than what? Than restoration?
1672: The therapist teaches her to breathe. It sounds ridiculous. But when he asks her to breathe slowly, making the exhale twice as long as the inhale, she realizes she doesn't know how. Within thirty seconds, she wants to inhale sooner.
1673: “Why is it so difficult?”—“Your nervous system is accustomed to a fast tempo. Slow breathing is a signal to the system: all is well, there is no danger. But if the system has believed for many years that danger is present—it does not trust this signal right away.”
1674: Marina breathes. Once more. Again. Gradually—a little easier.
1717: Marina begins to walk. Not to ‘hit a step count’—just to walk, wherever she wants, without headphones. The first few days it seems pointless. Then it begins to seem necessary.
1718: While walking, thoughts vanish as if in a dream. Then, if they do come, they are good thoughts, unlike thoughts, almost without words. Not anxious.
1719: It’s physiology. Rhythmic movement activates the serotonergic system, lowers cortisol levels, stimulates neurogenesis in the hippocampus. The brain in motion works differently.—“Differently—better?”—“Differently—more freely.”
1720:
Marina thinks about the book while walking. Not about the fear of the book. About the book itself. What it could be about.

Her feet on the asphalt set a rhythm for her thoughts, a slow, steady meter. The autumn air, sharp and clean, seems to carve out a space in her mind, a hollow where an idea could lodge. She does not force it. She simply walks, and the movement becomes a form of thinking.

A woman on a bridge, perhaps. Not looking at the water below, but at the city stretching away. A decision hanging in the balance, not of life and death, but of a different kind of courage. The plot is less important than the texture of the light, the weight of the silence, the specific ache of a choice not yet made. Marina walks, and the book begins, quietly, to walk beside her.
1768: A friend sends her an ad for a “detox program”: seven days, special juices, “cleansing the body of toxins.” Marina forwards it to her therapist with a single question: “Worth it?”
1769: The answer: "Your liver works around the clock and manages just fine without juices. The real toxic burden is poor nutrition, alcohol, ultra-processed food, chronic stress, and lack of sleep."
1770: Marina looks at the list. Chronic stress. It hasn't gone anywhere. Because its cause hasn't gone anywhere.
1819: The test reveals a significant vitamin D deficiency. And magnesium—hovering at the lower edge of normal.
1820: “Does this affect fatigue?”—“Vitamin D is essentially a hormone. It plays a role in regulating immunity, mood, energy metabolism. During chronic stress, magnesium is depleted faster than usual.”
1821: "So, I just take vitamins and get better?"—"It's like pouring oil into an engine that's long been running without it. Ideally, you should expose your face to the morning sun outside for 20-40 minutes, take 6-9 almonds as a safe method of regulating vitamin D and magnesium, and garnish your food with greens. But that is not enough—if the engine itself is malfunctioning. All reactions in the body are protective, including deficiencies in microelements or vitamins. You cannot forcibly suppress the body's defenses; you must find the cause of the causes and correct it."
1866: Marina prepares breakfast. Nuts, something green, a glass of water. A year ago she never thought about this—she ate on the run, between editing manuscripts. Now these are the first ten minutes of the day that belong to her alone. The therapist said: the body learns to trust when it sees regularity. Marina isn’t sure she believes that. But she notices: on the mornings when there is breakfast, the first half of the day passes differently. More quietly, somehow.
1918: Marina lies down on the osteopath’s table. The doctor places his hands on her shoulders—simply holds them. He does nothing—only holds.
1919: A minute later, Marina realizes she is crying. Without reason, without a thought. She is simply—crying.
1920: Your shoulders are carrying a great deal. They seem to be raised—a state of constant readiness. Is that how you always are?—I suppose so.
1921: “Try letting them drop.” Marina tries. It takes effort—to relax. Effort for relaxation. Her body knows no other way.
1970: Marina begins to meditate. Ten minutes each morning. The first few days: "I'm bad at this. The thoughts won't stop."
1971: The goal is not to stop your thoughts. The goal is to notice they are there, and to return. Each return is a training of the neural pathways responsible for regulating attention.
1972: A month later, Marina notices: she has begun to notice her own reactions sooner. Before they become automatic. It is a small, but real, change.
2022: Marina was skeptical—and said so outright: "I find it hard to take talk of biofields seriously. I can't touch it."
2023: This is a healthy skepticism. Let's sort it out honestly: what here is established fact, what is being actively researched, and what remains, for now, only a hypothesis.
2024: This was one of many conversations where Marina was told the plain, unadorned truth. Without any attempt to convince her of something more than what was. That, in itself, was a small act of healing. The trust placed in honesty.
2035: Note: The HeartMath Institute actively promotes this data. As a commercial organization, it primarily publishes its own research; independent MCG laboratories confirm the field is measurable, while its biological significance remains an open question.
2065: Marina saw her “health map” for the first time—a diagram her therapist had compiled over their months of work. It was not a list of diagnoses. It was a network: showing which nodes were connected, where the strain was concentrated, where a reserve of strength remained.
2066:
Is this me? she asks, looking at the diagram.

The endocrinologist has drawn it for her: a constellation of ovals and arrows. Thyroid, pituitary, adrenals, gut, brain. All linked. A tremor in one sends a shock through the whole.

For twenty years she has treated her life as a series of isolated failures. The abandoned novel. The strained friendships. The weary body. Separate files in separate drawers.

Now she sees the lines connecting them. The fear that silenced her pen also tightened her muscles, soured her stomach, whispered to her immune system. A single, reverberating signal.

The body, it turns out, keeps a meticulous record. It never forgets a thing.
2067:
“You are a system,” the therapist replies.

Marina sits with this. A system. Not a broken machine with a single faulty part to be replaced. A living, breathing network of cause and effect, of memory and muscle, of thought and tissue. Her fatigue is not an isolated malfunction. It is a signal from the whole.

She thinks of her silent thyroid, her vigilant immune cells, the old, cold fear lodged in her chest. They are not separate. They speak to one another in a language of hormones and whispers. The thought is unsettling. It means there is no single enemy to defeat. But it is also a relief. It means she is not a collection of broken pieces. She is a conversation. And a conversation can change.
2113:
Marina asks the therapist, “Do you ever feel tired of your work?”

The question hangs in the air, simple and sharp. It is not about medicine. It is about the weight of listening, the exhaustion of holding other people’s broken pieces day after day. She watches the woman’s face, the careful professional mask. For a moment, Marina sees not a doctor, but a person who might also be weary. The silence stretches, thin and diagnostic. In asking, Marina realizes she is no longer just a patient seeking an answer. She is offering a kind of recognition, a thread of shared fatigue. The room holds its breath, waiting to see if the thread will be taken or cut.
2114: Sometimes, he says honestly. That's why I monitor my own condition as closely as I monitor my patients'. A doctor who does not care for himself cannot truly care for others.
2115: Marina thinks: it’s true for any profession. For an editor. For a mother.
2156:
“I want to try something you might not approve of,” Marina says. She names the method.

The endocrinologist looks at her, not with the expected disapproval, but with a weary, professional curiosity. “I see. And what do you expect from it?”

Marina had prepared for a fight, for a lecture on evidence-based medicine. This calm question disarms her. She thinks of the acupuncturist’s quiet room, the smell of moxa, the promise of listening to the body rather than just its numbers.

“A different kind of attention,” she finally answers. “Not a cure. Just… attention.”

The doctor nods slowly, makes a note in her file. “Try it. Just keep taking your thyroxine. And tell me, in three months, what that attention taught you.” The permission, conditional and clinical, feels like a gift. Marina walks out, holding the slip of paper with the practitioner’s address, feeling not like a traitor to science, but like an explorer of her own silent terrain.
2157: The therapist listens. “I don’t think this will help you, and here is why. But it is your choice. Would you like to discuss the risks?”
2158:
For the first time in a doctor’s office, she is spoken to as an adult.

The endocrinologist, a woman with calm eyes, lays out the options like a menu. She explains the standard protocol, then speaks of diet, of stress, of sleep as legitimate, coequal factors. There is no pressure, only possibility. The hierarchy dissolves. Marina is not a vessel of malfunctioning hormones to be corrected, but a participant in a conversation about a body, her body.

She leaves with a prescription, yes, but also with a list of foods to consider and a time for her next appointment. The paper in her hand feels different. It does not dictate; it suggests. On the metro home, she holds the feeling close, this novel sensation of being an ally, rather than a hostage, to her own flesh.
2204: A regular client was dying—suddenly, from a heart attack, at fifty-two. Marina arrived for her appointment in a state of shock.
2205: He so wanted to finish his book, she says. He kept putting it off.
2206:
The therapist is silent. Marina can hear her own words hanging in the air.

She had just said, "I think I am letting my body die so I don't have to write." The sentence, spoken aloud, felt both absurd and profoundly true. It was not a wish for death, but a recognition of a slow, quiet surrender. A palliative care for a still-living dream.

She had been treating her life as a terminal condition, managing the symptoms of her existence—the fatigue, the brain fog, the ache—without ever addressing the cause. She was making herself comfortable in a life she was not really living. The silence of the room was not judgment; it was space, an opening she had just carved for herself.
2246: Marina’s friend was undergoing cancer treatment. Marina sat beside her during the IV drip and read aloud. Not medical literature—Chekhov.
2247: Later, she thinks: this is also therapy. The words, the voice, the presence. It is not instead of chemotherapy. But it is together with it.
2290: Marina’s father has a heart attack. She goes to the hospital and notices, for the first time: the doctors speak of vessels, of stents, of statins. No one asks how he has lived these last years.
2291: The heart as a metaphor, she writes in her journal that evening. And as biology. Simultaneously.
2331: Marina thinks, for the first time, about speaking to a psychotherapist. She resists the idea for a long time. Then she makes the appointment.
2332: She cries for almost the entire first session. “Is this normal?” she asks. “It means the work has begun,” the therapist replies.
2385:
Finally—an understanding of the mechanism of her diagnosis. Hashimoto's thyroiditis: the immune system attacks the thyroid gland.

Marina reads this and feels a strange, quiet relief. It is not a vague malaise, not a character flaw. It is a civil war within her own body. Her defenses have turned against her. She thinks of the manuscript in her drawer, untouched for years, and of the countless polite, devastating rejections. She sees the parallel now. Her own fierce, protective mechanisms, meant to shield her from failure, have instead besieged the very source of her energy. The gland that governs metabolism, that sets the body's tempo, is under assault. It explained the profound fatigue, the slowing of all things. Her body, in its misguided loyalty, was silencing her.
2386: Why does the immune system attack its own? That, we do not fully know. But stress, gut dysbiosis, genetic predisposition—all of these play a part.
2387:
Marina thinks: the body wages war against itself. She has long known this sensation—not only in the body.

It is the same internal conflict that has kept her from her desk for twenty years. One part of her, a fierce and willful commander, demands the assault: sit, write, conquer the page. The other part, a terrified conscript, digs trenches, erects barricades, surrenders without a fight. The fatigue, the brain fog, the aching joints—they are not just symptoms. They are the ceasefire in a civil war, a profound and exhausting stalemate. Peace, she begins to understand, will not come from one side annihilating the other. It will come from a negotiation she has spent a lifetime avoiding.
2425: Marina reads about insulin resistance and thinks of her mother. Her mother is prediabetic. Her mother eats little, hardly moves, sleeps poorly, is anxious always.
2426: Each of these factors, taken alone, seems a trifle. Together, they form a system that slowly dismantles the body.
2427: For the first time, Marina thinks of her mother not with the usual irritation, but with something akin to compassion.
2472: Marina thinks: medicine is growing more precise, faster, more powerful. But no algorithm will ever ask the question: “What did you want to write when you were twenty-three, and why did you stop?”
2473: Technology is a tool. Medicine is the meeting of one person with another. One without the other is incomplete.
2523: A single cell, Marina thinks, studying the printouts her therapist gave her. Smarter than anything I have ever edited.
2524: She looks at the diagram: mitochondria, nucleus, membrane receptors. A cell receives signals from its environment, processes them, responds. Every cell in her body is doing this right now—billions of simultaneous conversations about which she knows nothing.
2525: At her last appointment, the therapist had said, “When you understand how a cell is built, you begin to understand why what you eat, how you sleep, and what you think—these are not metaphors, but literal biochemistry.” Marina writes it down in her notebook. Then she adds: “So everything I do to myself is information I am sending to each one of them.”
2572: Marina looks at the MRI of her brain. Gray and white. Somewhere in there lives the fear. And somewhere—the desire to write. They live side by side.
2573: The neurologist had explained it to her a month ago: “The nervous system isn’t wiring. It’s a dynamic network, constantly rebuilding itself.” Marina hadn’t fully understood then. Now she understood differently: the pathways that lead to fear are not a life sentence. They are worn deep, like a forest trail. But there are others nearby. They can be blazed.
2574: Neuroplasticity—that was the word she now held in her mind when the fear came. A brain that had learned fear could learn something else. This was not a consolation. It was neurobiology.
2626: The immune system remembers everything, Marina thinks, reading the chapter a second time. A pity it doesn’t forget what has long since ceased to be a threat.
2627: Hashimoto’s thyroiditis is the immune system attacking its own thyroid gland. Marina had always understood this as “the body made a mistake.” Now she reads it differently: the immune system learned a lesson—that something was dangerous. And it keeps reacting as if that threat were still here.
2628: What threat had she internalized? What had triggered this lesson? The therapist says: these are questions worth asking not only of an immunologist, but of oneself. Marina begins to ask.
2674: For the first time in three years, her TSH was normal. 3.1. Marina stared at the printout for a long time.
2675: The endocrinologist says, “Excellent results. Keep going in the same direction.” Marina nods. But she is thinking of something else: the onset of her illness three years ago coincided with the moment she felt the desire to write most acutely—and was most clearly terrified. Now, as the fear has begun to dissipate, her hormones have also leveled out. This is not a coincidence. It is a conversation between systems.
2676: The hormonal orchestra, she would read in this chapter. Now she knew who the conductor had been. Not the thyroid gland. Something deeper.
2727: The border between you and the world, her therapist had said of the intestine. Ninety percent of serotonin is produced here. The gut speaks to the brain constantly—and it does far more of the talking than the brain ever does in return.
2728: Marina has been thinking about this for days. Her border had been closed too long—in both directions. Closed to what might have nourished her. Open to what only troubled and drained.
2729: She notices: when she thinks about the book—about finally starting to write her own—something clenches in her abdomen. Not a metaphor. Literally—a spasm. Her intestines respond to the thought faster than her head can process it. Her body knew all along. It was just waiting for her to start listening.
2772: One hundred thousand beats a day. Each one—without her participation. Her heart works even when she forgets about it—even when she fears failure, even when she edits other people's manuscripts instead of writing her own.
2773: On the biofeedback device, Marina sees the variability of her heart rate. The moment she thinks about the fear of rejection, the line becomes chaotic. The moment the therapist asks her to recall something worth being healthy for, the line evens out.
2774: The heart is a second brain, the therapist says. Marina thinks: perhaps it was the heart that first sensed she wanted to live differently. Long before it became words.
2817: The body is a physical object. And at the same time—it is more than a physical object. Marina is learning to live with this paradox. And, strangely, it calms her.
2818: She used to divide the world into the "real" and the "unreal." Anxiety—unreal, invented. Fatigue—real, biochemical. Now she understands: anxiety is biochemistry, too. Only it is biochemistry triggered by a thought. The physics of a thought is the physics of the body.
2819: A therapist once said: "The difference between the physical and the psychological is administrative. In the organism itself, that border does not exist." Marina holds this phrase like a compass: when they tell her again, "it's your nerves," she already knows it is not a dismissal. It is merely an incomplete explanation.
2859: MRI, CT, ultrasound—all of these see structure. But they do not see meaning. Marina had undergone enough of these examinations to know: they answer the question ‘what is there,’ but not the question ‘why did it appear’ or ‘what is to be truly done about it.’
2860: She remembers the MRI of her thyroid three years ago. The nodules, the structure, the size—all described with precision. But the report says nothing about how that same year, for the first time in twenty years, she thought again of writing her own book—and was so frightened she couldn't sleep for a week.
2861: The tools are important. They rule out what must be ruled out. But they are not a substitute for the question, "How do you live?"—and they never will be.
2911: Marina reads the chapter twice. Then a third time.
2912: She was accustomed to dividing the world into the "proven" and the "charlatan." Quantum biology was something else entirely: a field where serious scientists studied phenomena that did not yet fit into familiar explanations. Marina, who had worked with texts her whole life and knew the difference between "we do not know" and "this does not exist," read with curiosity, free from anxiety.
2913: The therapist had said, "The medicine of the future will be different not because it will find new drugs. But because it will ask different questions." Marina thinks: this chapter is about those questions we are only just learning to ask.
2950: A dissipative structure maintains order through a constant exchange with its environment, reads Marina. She reads the phrase again. Then writes it down in her notebook.
2951: A living system cannot be closed. A closed system moves toward chaos—that is the second law of thermodynamics. An open one exchanges, takes in, gives away—and it is precisely through this exchange that it maintains order. Marina thinks: for too long she has been a closed system. She has not let anything new inside. She has not released what has accumulated.
2952: The book she wants to write is not a whim or an ambition. It is a thermodynamic necessity. Her system wants to open. And her illness, perhaps, was the final argument in favor of the fact that one could no longer live like this.
2987: Marina notices: when she thinks of the book—her shoulders drop. When she thinks of the fear of rejection—they rise. Body and thought are one.
3035: Feedback. Regulation loops. Marina thinks: she herself is a feedback loop. What she receives from the world determines what she gives.
3086: Small changes yield large effects. Marina wrote three sentences this morning. It seems a small thing. Perhaps it is the beginning of something vast.
3135: Marina found her mother’s notebook in the archive. Notes from 1987—exercises from the sanatorium where her mother had been sent with an "asthenic syndrome." The handwriting was weary, the letters slumping downward toward the middle of the page. "Day 3. Swimming 20 min. Better." "Day 7. Back pain. Didn’t go out." "Day 12. Home."
3136: Marina closed the notebook and gazed out the window for a long time. The body does not merely fall ill. The body keeps a record. It keeps it more faithfully than we ever could.
3137:
The next morning, she went for a walk earlier than usual. Just because. Without a plan.

The air was different at this hour, sharp and unclaimed. The city had not yet settled into its daytime roar; sounds were distinct—the scrape of a broom on asphalt, the clang of a distant tram. Her own footsteps were a clear, solitary rhythm. She walked without destination, past sleeping storefronts and bleary-eyed kiosks, her breath forming small clouds that vanished instantly.

For the first time in months, perhaps years, there was no internal list of tasks, no grim tally of symptoms to monitor. Her body was simply moving through the cold, quiet world. And in that motion, in the emptiness of the early avenue, a thought arose, not as a demand, but as a quiet, physical fact: the body is not a fortress. It is a conversation.
3177: Signals. Everything is a signal. The body speaks constantly. Marina is finally learning to listen.
3220: At three in the morning, Marina is awake. Not from anxiety — from an idea. She has just understood how to begin the first chapter of her book. Not the one she was afraid to write for twenty years. Just — the first chapter. One.
3221: She does not get up. She knows: if she rises, her mind will switch on, and the idea will dissolve into the to-do list.
3222: She repeats the first sentence to herself. Again and again. Until she falls asleep.
3223:
In the morning, it was still there.

The fear. A cold, dense stone in her gut. She sat at her desk, the blank document open, the cursor blinking with a terrible, patient rhythm. For twenty years, this stone had been her lodger, her excuse, her illness’s most faithful ally. It had whispered that silence was safer. That to be sick was a valid reason not to try.

But now she knew its name. She saw its shape. It was not the illness itself, but the illness’s oldest, most cunning cause. A primitive alarm, screaming of a predator—the predator of failure, of exposure. Her body had listened to that alarm for decades, had shaped its defenses around it.

Her fingers hovered over the keys. The stone was there. But so was she.
3262: Mother. The grandmother Marina never knew. What did they carry inside themselves—what had reached her? And what was she passing on to her daughter? This question no longer frightened her. It motivated her.
3302: Proteins, DNA, lipids, carbohydrates. All of it—is her. Molecules that take the shape of a person. A person who wants to write.
3358:
Marina noticed: she had stopped apologizing for the pauses in conversation.

Before, each silence felt like a personal failure, a hole in the social fabric she was obliged to mend with a hasty “sorry” or “I’m just a bit tired.” Now, the pauses simply were. They hung in the air, neutral, belonging to no one. She let them breathe. In those quiet spaces, she could feel the faint, electrical hum of her own thoughts, a current long dampened by the static of apology. It was a small, seismic shift. The medicine, she supposed, was working—not just the synthetic hormone in her blood, but the other, more clandestine pharmacology of slowly reclaiming the territory of herself, word by silent word.
3359: Before, any silence had to be filled. To say something, to clarify, to explain. Now she sometimes simply stays silent. And she sees that her interlocutor also begins to think.
3360: It was a small thing. But she remembered how her therapist had said: change begins when you stop doing something habitual. You don't add something new—you remove the old.
3361: The fatigue today was a three out of ten. She recorded it in her diary. She added: "It seems this is my normal now."
3408: Free radicals attack cells. The body defends itself. Marina thinks: she, too, is finally beginning to defend herself. Truly. Not with illness—with choice.
3456:
The last test results: her inflammatory markers had dropped. A little. But they had dropped.

Marina sat with the printout in her lap, the numbers a quiet confirmation in black and white. For years, the cascade had felt unstoppable, a chemical fire raging through her veins, turning every thought to ash. Now, a single digit, shifted minutely in the right column, felt like a ceasefire.

It was not a cure. The fatigue still clung to her bones like a damp coat. But the internal storm was calming. In the new quiet, she could hear something else: not the roar of her own defenses, but the faint, persistent click of a keyboard in the next room, a sound that was no longer a reproach, but an invitation.
3457:
What had changed? Her diet. Her sleep. Her breathing. And something important—inside.

The cascade had quieted. The internal sirens, which had wailed for years at the slightest disturbance, now only murmured a low, manageable hum. It was not the silence of surrender, but the quiet of a watchful truce. Her body was no longer a fortress under siege, every system mobilizing against a phantom enemy. It was simply a place she inhabited again.

She noticed it in the mornings, in the absence of that leaden dread. In the afternoons, when her mind did not fog over like a frozen window. The chemistry of her reactions had altered. A setback was no longer a catastrophe; it was merely a piece of data. A criticism did not trigger a systemic shutdown. The inflammation, once a wildfire, was now a banked hearth. And in that steadier warmth, something long-dormant had begun, very faintly, to glow.
3505:
Marina asks, “Serotonin—does it really affect your mood?”

The doctor, a young woman with tired eyes behind thick glasses, looks up from her screen. She considers the question, not as a clinician reciting a textbook, but as if weighing the very substance of it. “It is a modulator,” she says finally. “A background condition. Like… the weather inside you. It does not create the storm, but it decides if the sky is clear or heavy with clouds when the storm arrives.”

Marina thinks of her own inner climate. The long, low-pressure fronts of fatigue. The sudden squalls of panic when she sits before a blank page. For years, she believed her biochemistry was the immutable landscape of her life. Now she wonders: if the weather can shift, however slightly, what else might change? What if the page is not a void, but a space waiting for its own particular light?
3506: It is more complex than "serotonin equals happiness." The neurochemistry of mood is an entire network. But this much is known for certain: nutrition, sleep, movement, social bonds, and a sense of meaning affect this system in measurable ways.
3555:
Marina reads the book to its final page.

She closes the cover. The silence in the room is not empty; it is thick, expectant. For twenty years, she had been a vessel for other people’s words, a careful curator of their finished worlds. Now, within her, something long dormant shifts and settles.

The diagnosis had been a kind of ending. The fatigue, a deep winter. But this feels different. This is not an end, but a clearing. The medicine was not just in the pills or the protocols, but in this slow, patient turning back toward herself.

Outside, a thin, persistent rain falls. She opens a new document on her screen. The cursor blinks on the blankness, a steady, patient pulse. She places her fingers on the keys. The journey continues.
3556: She closes it. Sits for several minutes in the silence.
3557: Then she opens a new document on her computer. At the top, she types a single word: "Chapter."
3558:
And she begins.

The cursor blinks on the empty screen, a tiny, patient lighthouse. For twenty years, it was a warning beacon, signaling the treacherous coast of her own insufficiency. Now, it is simply a pulse. A point of departure.

Her hands rest on the keyboard, no longer heavy with the old, familiar ache. The fatigue is still there, a low hum in her bones, but it is no longer the master of the house. It is just a fact, like the grey light of the Moscow morning at her window.

She does not think of chapters or plots, of publishers or rejections. She thinks of a single sentence. The truest one she can find. She exhales, a long, quiet release of a breath held for two decades.

Her fingers move. A letter appears. Then another. The story, at last, is allowed to enter the room.
3598: An important clarification. The concept of "illness as a defensive reaction," presented in this epilogue, describes a specific class of clinical situations—primarily chronic functional and psychosomatic disorders, in which a physiological process is linked to an unresolved psychological conflict or a suppressed deep-seated striving. It is not applicable as a universal explanation: acute infections, injuries, oncological diseases, genetically determined conditions have their own mechanisms, not reducible to this model. The concept in no way implies that a patient "created" their own illness or bears moral responsibility for it. Its purpose is to expand the field of understanding, not to add a burden of guilt.
3656: Six months after she began to write, Marina came for an appointment. The endocrinologist, a young woman with a tired face, opened her file and then looked up, her gaze resting not on the chart, but on Marina herself. She asked not about antibodies, but about the book. She listened to the answer about structure and character, about the daily struggle with the blank page, as if it were a crucial symptom. She nodded, her pen still. For the first time in a decade of consultations, Marina felt the clinical distance dissolve. Here, in this sterile room, her creative life was not an irrelevant footnote to her illness, but its central, beating organ. The prescription was not altered. Yet everything was different. The medicine, finally, felt worthy of the human taking it.
3657: The fatigue is less, she said. It hasn't vanished. But it is less.
3658:
“What has changed?” the therapist asked.

Marina looked at the ceiling. A crack ran from the chandelier to the corner, a fine, dry riverbed. She had been coming here for months, speaking of her fatigue, the fog in her head, the weight in her limbs. She listed the new pills, the eliminated foods, the early bedtimes. A catalogue of adjustments.

“I walk,” she finally said. “To the bakery. Not the closest one. The one with the almond croissants.”

The therapist waited.

“It takes twenty-three minutes. I count the types of doorways. I feel the air. Sometimes I don’t buy anything. I just go back.”

She did not say: the walk is an empty page. The counting of doorways, a rhythm. The return, a sentence completed. It was not a cure. It was a line, drawn by her own feet, between one point and another. A thing measured not in milligrams, but in minutes and footsteps. A medicine made of motion and attention.
3659: She writes. Every morning—half an hour. She doesn’t yet know what will come of it. Perhaps nothing. But she writes—and she is not so afraid.
3660: “Why isn’t it so frightening?”—“Because I realized: my body wasn’t protecting me from writing. It was protecting me from facing another defeat without enough strength. Now I have a little more strength. Not much. But enough to try.”
3661: The therapist nodded. "That is enough," he said.
